coredata.dc:description: This paper explains the basis for the excitation energy-independent fluorescence emission of biomass-derived carbon dots (CDs) and shows that these CDs have excellent anti-melanoma and anti-metastatic potential. Additionally, we demonstrate that the black carrots´-derived CDs can be exploited as cell cycle-sensing agents, because of the interaction with chromatin material. Besides their optical properties, fluorescent CDs have gained increased attention for image-guided cancer treatment due to their water solubility, environmental friendliness, affordability, ease of synthesis, and primary biocompatibility. CDs have excellent photostability, determined by their precursors and synthesis pathways. In this study, CDs with chemically homogenous surface functional groups were made using a hydrothermal technique from black carrot extract, an anthocyanin-rich substance derived from biomass. The anti-cancer and anti-metastatic properties of black carrot-derived CDs can be attributed to flavylium cations on the surface, spherical forms, and high water dispersibility. Most importantly, these CDs demonstrate a stable emission at a single wavelength, 612 nm, independent of the excitation energy, which we have explained theoretically for the first time.

The identified anthocyanins are indicated by numbers: (1) cyanidin-3 - xylosyl - glucosyl - galactoside; (2) cyanidin-3-xylosyl -galactoside; (3) cyanidin-3-xylosyl-sinapoyl-glucosyl - galactoside; (4) cyanidin −3 – xylosyl - feruloyl-glucosyl galactoside; (5) cyanidin-3-xylosyl-coumaroyl-glucosyl-galactoside.

(a) Absorption spectra of CDs and the extract of black carrots. In (b), fluorescence spectra of CDs obtained upon the excitation ranging from 400-500 nm are shown.

Schematic representation of the energy levels involved in the ESIPT and ground state intramolecular proton transfer (GSIPT) processes of the hemiketal-chalcone coupled systems. Numbers on the horizontal lines represent the energy (in eV) of molecular species referred to in the enol form. Numbers on vertical arrows represent S0 → S1 vertical excitation energies. In parenthesis, we calculated energy values, including the solvent effect. *: geometries optimized in the ground state.

Energy profiles of potential energy surfaces leading (a) from (FC(1)) to Minimum M1a; (b) from (FC(2)) to Minimum M1b. The characters of the electronic configurations of the S1 and S2 states are indicated in each excited curve—CDs: Carbon Dot; fl: flavylium moiety.
Diagrammatic representation of the relaxation channels of the nanoparticle after excitation between 400 and 500 nm.

Viability (a,b,c, and d) of MRC-5, PANC-1, HeLa, and A375 cells after the treatment with indicated CD concentrations, respectively. An asterisk indicates the significant differences in the viability (**p < 0.01, ****p < 0.0001), and n.s. indicates a lack of a statistically significant difference.

Analysis of A375 cell migration by in vitro wound healing assay. (a) Representative microscopy images of the wound closure in the A375 untreated control cells and cells treated with CDs (0.053 mgmL−1) at 0 h time point and after 24 h incubation time. Scale bars, 100 µm. (b) Quantitative analysis of the scratch wound healing assay after 24 h.

CDs located in A375 cells recorded with 20 × magnification (e),(f),(g), (h)and 100X magnification (i),(j), (k) and (l). The first panel shows a blue channel (DAPI), the second panel shows a green channel the third panel shows inverted DAPI staining, and the fourth panel shows merged channels. White arrows indicate cells with the highest fluorescence in different stages of mitosis. Scale bar = 50 µm (a-h) and 10 µm (i-l).

The maximum emission intensity changes with a different exposure time of UV light (365 nm). n.s. indicates a lack of a statistically significant difference. An asterisk indicates the significant differences in the fluorescent maximum intensity (**p < 0.01) compared to the intensity before the UV exposure (0 min).

ce:abstract-sec.ce:simple-para: This paper explains the basis for the excitation energy-independent fluorescence emission of biomass-derived carbon dots (CDs) and shows that these CDs have excellent anti-melanoma and anti-metastatic potential. Additionally, we demonstrate that the black carrots´-derived CDs can be exploited as cell cycle-sensing agents, because of the interaction with chromatin material. Besides their optical properties, fluorescent CDs have gained increased attention for image-guided cancer treatment due to their water solubility, environmental friendliness, affordability, ease of synthesis, and primary biocompatibility. CDs have excellent photostability, determined by their precursors and synthesis pathways. In this study, CDs with chemically homogenous surface functional groups were made using a hydrothermal technique from black carrot extract, an anthocyanin-rich substance derived from biomass. The anti-cancer and anti-metastatic properties of black carrot-derived CDs can be attributed to flavylium cations on the surface, spherical forms, and high water dispersibility. Most importantly, these CDs demonstrate a stable emission at a single wavelength, 612 nm, independent of the excitation energy, which we have explained theoretically for the first time.

ce:list-item.ce:para: The raw/processed data required to reproduce these findings cannot be shared at this time due to technical or time limitations.

ce:section.ce:para: Nanoscale materials derived from natural sources, such as biomass-based carbon nanomaterials, show desirable properties as anti-cancer agents [1]. Fabrication of carbon dots (CDs), a form of nanocarbon, from waste biomass or other organic precursors is simple and economical [2,3]. The variations in the core and surface states of the synthesized CDs lead to a broad diversity of structures and behaviors. Low cost, eco-friendliness, an abundance of functional groups (e.g., amine, hydroxyl, and carboxyl), and high stability make them attractive candidates for biomedical applications, including bioimaging, phototherapy, and anti-cancer treatment [4].

CDs have been the subject of intense studies because of their excellent fluorescent emission and sensing properties [5–8]. Particulary studies with CDs derived from biowaste for cell imaging stand out because of the growing global biowaste and its environmental issues, which challenge the need for converting biowastes into beneficial imaging products [9–11]. Recent studies demonstrate DNA targeting and the genotoxic effect of CDs, which is mediated by increased reactive oxygen species production [12,13]. These properties made CDs a promising s for traditional organic dyes in fluorescent bioimaging [14,15]. However, up to now, the exact mechanism of fluorescence emission of CDs is controversial and requires further clarification. A few factors have been indicated governing this process, including quantum size [16–19], surface defects [3,20,21], or molecular states [22–25]. One possible explanation for the CDs' emission mechanism involves oxidation and various surface functional groups existing on their surfaces [3]. Another points out a quantum effect of hole-electron recombination, analogous to QDs semiconductor systems [26].

Because of their high anthocyanin (ACN) content, black carrots were selected as precursors of CDs in this study. ACNs are polyphenolic compounds belonging to the flavonoid group [27], which have attracted interest in recent decades as potential anti-cancer agents [28–30]. These compounds are structurally based on the polyhydroxy or polymethoxy derivatives of 2-phenylbenzopyrylium (flavylium ion) and appear as glycosides and acyl glycosides of their respective aglycones (anthocyanidins). Anthocyanidins are structurally composed of a C6C3C6 carbon skeleton. They consist of a benzopyrylium core (an aromatic ring A bound to a heterocyclic ring C) attached at C2 to an aromatic ring B (formed by condensing a p-coumaroyl-CoA with three molecules of malonyl CoA) (Fig. 1 ). Due to their instability, anthocyanidins are converted to O-glycosylation immediately after synthesis (by a glycosidic bond between a corresponding sugar moiety and a C3 position of a C ring).

Sugar residues can also be acylated with aliphatic or aromatic acids, giving better stability in their chemical structure. Thus, hydrocarbon residues of this family of polyphenols were expected to give rise to CDs decorated with OH and C=O groups. Such chemistry was targeted following our hypothesis that states that this specific chemical environment results in the independence of fluorescence at the excitation wavelength. One of the objectives of this study was to obtain a particular degree of chemical homogeneity on the CDs' surface from an unexploited anthocyanin-rich source and, based on their fluorescence properties and surface features, to clarify the fluorescence mechanism and to explore their ability for cancer treatment and medical imaging.

A hydrothermal process was applied to obtain carbon nanoparticles (CDs) from black carrot extract. The details of the extraction procedure are given in the Supplementary Information (SI). Briefly, the black carrot extract (25 mL, Figure SI.1) was transferred to a Teflon-lined stainless-steel reactor, mixed with trifluoracetic acid (TFA, 99%), and then treated for 4 h in an oven at 120 ºC (Scheme 1 ).

Trifluoroacetic acid, TFA, was added to dissolve the extract and promote oxidation. After the TFA addition, the obtained solution was left at room temperature and filtered through a 0.22 µm membrane filter to remove the sediment. The residual solution was further purified by dialysis process (MWCO of dialysis membrane was 500 Da) v. H2O, which was fluorescent upon the UV light illumination.

The morphology of CDs was analyzed by transmission electron microscopy (TEM) on a FEI Talos F200X. XPS analysis was performed on a Physical Electronics PHI VersaProbe II spectrometer using monochromatic Al-Kα radiation (49.1 W, 15 kV, and 1486.6 eV) with a hemispherical multichannel detector. Spectra were recorded with a constant pass energy value at 29.35 eV, using a 200 µm diameter circular analysis area. The XPS spectra were analyzed using PHI SmartSoft software and processed using the MultiPak 9.3 package. The binding energy values were referenced to the adventitious carbon C 1s signal (284.8 eV). Shirley-type background and Gauss-Lorentz curves were used to determine the binding energies. Fluorometric measurements were performed with a commercial Hitachi F-4500 spectrofluorometric equipped with a 150 W Xenon lamp and interfaced to a PC for instrument operation and spectra processing. The slit width was adjusted to 2.5 nm for excitation and emission, and the photomultiplier (PMT R-3788 Vage was set at 950 V. The ratio of areas of the fluorescence spectra of solutions with matched absorbances at the excitation wavelength, which corresponds to concentrations of the carbon nanoparticles, was used to calculate the fluorescence quantum yields (ΦFL) at room temperature relative to an appropriate reference compound, as standard was used fluorescein with ΦFL = 0.95, which was dissolved in sodium hydroxide. The results were corrected for refractive index when using water as the solvent.

The quantification of anthocyanins was performed on an HPLC system (1200 Series; Agilent Technologies Inc., Palo Alto, CA, USA), coupled with a diode array detector (the eluent was monitored at 520 nm), following the protocol described elsewhere [31]. The methanolic extract of black carrots was briefly filtered (0.45 µm) before an injection. The separation of different anthocyanins was performed on a polymer reversed-phase column (PLRP-S; 250 × 4.6 mm, 5 µm particle size, 100 Å pore size; Varian, Palo Alto, CA, US) at 25 °C by applying a linear gradient at 0.8 mL/min. The starting conditions were 95% solvent A (Milli-Q water with 0.1% formic acid), 5% solvent B (acetonitrile with 0.1% formic acid); at 60 min, 71% solvent A, 29% solvent B; at 62 min, 25% solvent A, 75% solvent B, and at 69 min the conditions were restored to an initial setting. The anthocyanin profile of the extract was determined by comparison with a previous study performed by our group [32], where anthocyanins were identified using an HPLC system fitted to a time-of-flight (TOF) mass spectrometer. The quantification of anthocyanins was performed based on the calibration curves of cyanidin-3-O-glucoside. The total anthocyanin content was calculated as the sum of the individual anthocyanin concentrations and expressed as mg/g based on a dry weight (dw) of cyanidin-3-O-glucoside.

For a more precise interpretation of experimental results, quantum chemical calculations were performed with the density functional theory (DFT) using the hybrid exchange-correlation functional CAM-B3LYP [33] in conjunction with the def2-SVPP and def2-TZVPP basis sets [34,35] as they have implemented in the Gaussian program [36]. The geometries of all species were optimized in redundant internal coordinates with the polarizable continuum model of Tomasi to simulate the effect of the solvent [37–41]. Analytical frequencies were performed on each optimized structure to ensure they were stationary points on the potential energy surfaces. In addition, we have studied the excited state intramolecular proton transfer (ESIPT) leading to keto-enol isomerization Scheme 2 [3,42–46].

Three human cancer cell lines, including melanoma A375 (CRL-1619), cervical carcinoma HeLa (CCL-2), and pancreatic carcinoma PANC-1 (CRL-1469), as well as MRC-5 (CCL-171) human lung fibroblasts, were obtained from the American Type Culture Collection (ATCC, Manassas, VA, USA). All the cells were cultured in Dulbecco's Modified Eagle Medium (DMEM) with 4500 mgL−1 glucose and l-glutamine (Biowest, Nuaillé - France), supplemented with 10% fetal bovine serum (FBS, PAN-Biotech, Aidenbach, Germany), 10,000 UmL−1 penicillin and 10 mgmL−1 streptomycin solution (Capricorn Scientific, Germany). They were maintained under standard conditions at 37 °C in a humidified atmosphere with 5% CO2 (Esco Lifesciences Group, Singapore, Singapore).

The biological activity of CDs against different types of tumor cell lines (A375, HeLa, and PANC-1) and non-malignant cell line MRC-5 was assessed by the sulforhodamine B (SRB) assay [47,48]. Cell lines were seeded in 96-well plates (Thermo Fischer Scientific, Waltham, MA, USA) (2 × 103 cells/well for A375, MRC-5, HeLa, and 3 × 103 cells/well for PANC-1) overnight and then treated with CDs at concentrations of 0.0131–0.221 mgmL−1. The concentration range was selected to ensure an optimal capture of the dose-response range of NPs in the cell lines of interest. After 48 h, cell viability was assessed by SRB assay. The absorbance was measured at 550 nm with a reference at 690 nm in a microplate reader (Wallac, VICTOR2 1420 Multilabel counter, PerkinElmer, Turku, Finland). The experiments were made in triplicate and repeated at least three times. The results were expressed as a percent of cell viability determined according to the following equitation:
Cell

The obtained data were presented as the Mean ± SD (standard deviation). The significance of differences among the experimental groups was assessed by the independent 'Student's t-test, with p < 0.05. IC50 (half-maximal inhibitory concentration values) was calculated from each cell line's dose-response curves. The selectivity index (SI), which indicates the cytotoxic selectivity (i.e., potential drug safety) for CDs against cancer cells versus normal cells, was calculated from the following formula:

A375 cells were seeded at 500 000 cells/well in 6-well plates (Thermo Fischer Scientific, Waltham, MA, USA) and left overnight to create a confluent monolayer. Cells were then scraped with a 200 µL sterile pipette tip and extensively washed with PBS to remove detached cells. Fresh medium without FBS was added to the wells. For treated cells, 0.053 mgmL−1 of CDs were resuspended in the medium and added to the cells. Each well was instantly centered and imaged at 100 × magnification using a Zeiss Axiovert 200 microscope equipped with a camera. After 24 h, images of the same areas were acquired.

Cells were grown overnight as a monolayer on polylysine-coated slides (Merck, Germany) and treated with 0.221 mgmL−1 CDs at 37 °C. After 3 h incubation, cells were briefly washed with a PBS solution, fixed in 4% formaldehyde solution in PBS for 15 min, dehydrated in a series of ethanol solutions (70%, 95%, and 99.9%) and counterstained with DAPI-Vectashield solution (Vector Laboratories, United Kingdom). Coverslips were placed on polylysine-coated glass slides, and samples were analyzed using an epifluorescent Zeiss-Axioimager A1 microscope (Carl Zeiss, Jena, Germany) and the ISIS imaging software package (MetaSystems, Altlussheim, Germany).
Statistical tests, One-way ANOVA, were performed using GraphPad Prism, v.7 software. A p-value of less than 0.05 indicates significant differences between the datasets.

The total anthocyanin content of black carrots was 8.70±0.04 mg/g of dry matter, which is within the range reported in the literature [49–52] and higher than the values for well-known anthocyanin-rich matrices such as raspberries, blueberries, or cherries [53]. The ACNs profile of black carrots (Fig. 2 ) consists of 5 major cyanidin-based anthocyanins, differing in the nature and number of sugar units bound to the structure (all of them in the 3-OH position) as well as in the presence of aromatic acids linked to the sugar moieties.

The tri-glycoside cyanidin-3-xylosyl-glucosyl-galactoside (peak 1) and the di-glycosidic cyanidin-3-xylosyl-galactoside (peak 2) represent the non-acylated ACNs of black carrots. The compounds with an acylated structure, which constitute 89.0% of the total anthocyanins content in black carrots, are sinapoyl-(peak 3), feruloyl-(peak 4), and p-coumaroyl- (peak 5) derivatives of the tri-glycoside mentioned above cyanidin. The predominant ACNs in black carrots correspond to cyanidin-3-xylosyl-feruloyl-glucosylgalactoside, representing around 83.5% of total anthocyanins. This profile agrees with previously reported data [33,51–56] and highlights black carrots as the primary natural sources of acylated ACNs, which are more stable than their non-acylated counterparts [32].

A representative TEM micrograph and a 3D representation of the CDs are given in Fig. 3 . The particles are well dispersed in water, exhibit regular globular shapes with sizes of about 30 nm, and have a typical amorphous carbonaceous structure of no discernible lattice, as observed in CDs synthesized from several sources, among others, from lactose as a precursor [57].

The average size of the CDs is typically below 10 nm, although larger size CDs have also been reported. The synthesized CDs from natural products may vary in size and range from ultra-small size, about 1 nm diameter up to 70 nm, depending on the synthesis precursor [58]. However, Liu and co-workers reported the 60 nm diameter CDs synthesis procedure from graphene [59]. Bajpai and co-workers have used Guar Gum as the precursor to prepare CDs that had diameters in the range of 20–30 nm [60]. Moreover, Pete et al. report CDs fabricated from Annona squamosa (L.) peels with a size range of up to 40 nm [61]. In addition, Kasibabu and co-workers synthesized N-doped CDs with 70 nm using a hydrothermal approach from Papaya juice [62].We hypothesize that anthocyanins contribute the larger size of our CDs.However, CDs exhibit a green fluorescence under UV light (Scheme 1).

XPS was used to analyze the surface of CDs, and the resulting spectra showed that the surface of CDs contains the following elements (in atomic composition, at%), C: 56.02; O: 37%; F: 3.1; N: 2.1 and traces of K and P (Figure SI2). The deconvolution of C 1 s core level spectra provides essential information on the quality and quantity of carbon-oxygen functional groups (Fig. 4 A). Although the basic definition of CDs indicates a higher content of carbons, there are also applicable CDs containing lower percentages of carbon reported [57]

The following contributions were found: 284.8 eV (-C-C-/-C=C- (aromatic): 56%, 286.2 eV (C-O-C/C-OH/C-N): 23%, 287.44 eV (-C=O): 7% and at 288.8 eV for carboxylate (O-C=O): 14% [55]. Thus, most carbon-oxygen bonds are in C-OH, C-O-C, and O-C=O groups, as anticipated based on the chemical content of the extract and confirming in part the presence of the flavylium ion. Based on the XPS results, the cumulative amount of these bonds is about 21%, which we consider a substantial contribution to total carbon bonds. F arises from TFA added to the solution (Fig. 4B). Fluor is bound covalently, as the contribution at 687.9 eV can be assigned to the covalent C-F bond of TFA (93%) and via electrostatic interactions, seen through the weak contribution of F at 684.31 eV (7%). Even though the contributions of F and N were tiny, the F 1 s and N 1 s core energy level spectra were deconvolved. N is present as amine (at 399.5 eV) and protonated species (alkylammonium) at 401.5 eV (Fig. 4C) [56]. The O 1 s deconvolution shows that the C=O contribution at 530.5 eV from carbonyl and carboxylate groups, the last one mainly from TFA, is more than twice more extensive than that of C-O (Scheme 2, Fig. 4D).

The XPS analysis results discussed above suggested that the synthesis process applied to obtain CDs resulted in the specific functional groups on their surface, namely CO-C/COH in flavylium cations, which are expected to dominate the fluorescence emission process [64]. In the UV–Vis spectra of the CDs aqueous solution (Fig. 5 a), the peak at 280 nm could be ascribed to the π → π* transitions of aromatic sp2 domains on the surface of CDs and, at 347 nm to the others n → π* transitions with a moderate absorption. Fig. 5b presents the fluorescence spectrum of CDs obtained by treating the extract of ACNs with concentrated TFA in an aqueous medium upon excitation at different wavelengths (from 400 to 500 nm). Given the relevance of the fluorescence quantum yield (ΦF) of the generated carbon nanoparticles, in our experiments, it was Φ F = 0.13. It was interesting that compared to other similar flavylium cation analogs of anthocyanins [65], is in accordance with the measurements obtained, which concluded that the presence of hydroxyl‑derivates, such as the presence of sugars moieties decrease the ΦF.
The fluorescence spectra clearly show that upon an increase in the excitation wavelength, the emission maximum remains unchanged and gives a peak at 612 nm.

Based on the surface composition of the CDs, this redshift, compared to the emission of most CDs, can be linked to governing the fluorescence response by a single functional group [66]. Our first attempt to explain the fluorescence results was based on an excited state intramolecular proton transfer (ESIPT) phenomenon. The conditions are rigorous for this process to occur. One of the requirements is the presence of electron donor groups (usually OH, NH, or S-H) on the surface of CDs. An acceptor must be located at a close molecular distance to the donor, and both should be connected by an H-bond [67]. This condition is fulfilled in Scheme 2, in a hemiketal moiety, a part of the extract of the ACNs as a precursor for CDs. Nevertheless, the specific excitation energy must be supplied to ensure the proton transfer process.

ce:section.ce:section-title: Theoretical explanation of the single emission wavelength
ce:section.ce:para: We performed the DFT chemical calculations to clarify the lack of dependence of the fluorescence spectra of black-carrot-derived CDs on the excitation wavelength and the excitation energy. This finding would help us to understand and derive the mechanism of the process. Since the experiments were performed at acidic pH values, a flavylium cation derivative is the dominant stable species on the CD's surface. Accordingly, the geometrical model to simulate the carbon framework of CDs nanoparticles consists of the flavylium cation covalently bonded to an anthracene moiety (Figure SI3). This model of CDs has already been successfully applied in recent publications [68–70], and it is selected to confirm the validity of our results [40,45].

With this molecular model, the absorption wavelengths of the first excited state (S1) for the hemiketal and chalcone forms (Scheme 2) predicted by the calculations are 364 nm (3.41 eV) and 386 nm (3.21 eV), respectively. Given that the highest energy radiation (shortest wavelength) applied in our experiments is 400 nm (3.10 eV), the existence of the ESIPT process must be discarded because the S1 excited states of the hemiketal or chalcone forms (Scheme 2) are not accessible even at the energetically most favorable wavelength used in this work. Furthermore, for ESIPT to occur, a small energy barrier should be surmounted, which, in turn, would require additional energy. This process is illustrated in Fig. 6 , where the energetics of the hemiketal-chalcone interconversion reactions in the excited and ground states are represented; GSIPT denotes ground state intramolecular proton transfer.

Once the ESIPT is eliminated as the fluorescence mechanism, we investigated the electronic properties of the flavylium cation, which, in turn, is the dominant species in an acid medium. The calculations predict that the vertical excitation energies of the S1 and S2 states for the flavylium cation lie at 547 and 438 nm, respectively (M0, Fig. 7 ), whose oscillator strengths are 0.0017 for the S1 and 0.05612 S2 state. Anthracene has been enlarged with two rings to give pentacene to validate the structural model simulating the aromatic skeleton of CDs. According to the results presented in Table S1, the excitation properties in the range of wavelengths used in the experiments are not significantly changed in passing from anthracene to pentacene. Therefore, following the energetics of the states involved in the process, the irradiation of the nanoparticles in the 400-500 nm wavelength range populates either the S1 or S2 state. Furthermore, it was found that there are two minima on the S1 potential energy surface (M1a and M1b) whose electronic configurations are quite different (the main molecular orbitals of the two minima on the S1 excited, which describe such electronic configurations, are represented in Figure SI3), M1a is characterized by a …(πCDs)1(π*fl)1 electronic configuration that agrees with the electronic configuration of the vertical excitation to S1 while M1b is characterized by (πfl)1(π*fl)1 electronic structure that correlates with the character of the electronic configuration of the vertical excitation to S2.

The fact that the electronic configuration of the S2 vertical excitation is the same as M1b on the S1 surface indicates a surface crossing through a conical intersection [71–77]. To illustrate this process, we have calculated the energy profiles that connect the S1 and S2 Franck-Condon points with the two minima on the S1 surface (M1a and M1b) (Fig. 7a,b) through an interpolation method [76–79]. Thus, Fig. 7a illustrates how, after photon absorption, the excitation to the first excited state (S1) at the Franck-Condon point [FC(1)] allows the system to reach the M1a minimum on the S1 surface via a descendent path without a barrier (Adiabatic path), as it is found in the geometry optimization starting at the Franck-Condon point of the S1 state. In contrast, the geometry optimization starting at the Franck-Condon point [FC(2)] in the S2 state ends at the M1b minimum (S1 state); in consequence, the interpolation curve [FC(2)-M1b] represented in Fig. 7b is interpreted as follows: after excitation to the second excited state (S2) at the Franck-Condon point [FC(2)], the system relaxes through a S2/S1 conical intersection (S2 → S1 deactivation) which ends at the M1b minimum (S1 state). In other words, S2 → S1 deactivation occurs via an internal conversion process.

Therefore, independent of the initially populated states (S1 or S2), the system decays non-radiatively to a local minimum energy point, either M1a or M1b, and both lie on the S1 surface. Afterward, the system deactivates radiatively from S1 to S0 via fluorescence emission either from M1a or M1b. Thus, our system is an example that fulfills the empirical Kasha rule. The calculated values for the vertical emission from M1a or M1b are 533 and 616 nm, and the oscillator strengths are 0.9120 and 0.0809, respectively (Scheme 3 ). It must be noted that the oscillator strengths of the calculations do not agree with the intensities of the experimental bands; that is, the intensities predicted by the calculated forces are opposed to the observed intensities.

The reason for this discrepancy might arise from the mechanism of the population of both emitting states. The most intense bands originated by relaxation of the S2 state to the minimum M1b. Given that the oscillator strength of the S2 state is two orders of magnitude greater than the excitation to S1, the M1b with lower oscillator strength has more population than M1a. Given that the calculations predicted two emissions from two different electronic states of the same moiety (cation flavylium) and these two states have different electronic configurations on the S1 surface, two fluorescence bands should have been revealed at their respective domains.

ce:section.ce:section-title: Cytotoxicity of black carrot-based carbon dots
ce:section.ce:para: The potential of black carrot-derived CDs as anti-cancer agents was evaluated by their incubation with three cancer cell lines, HeLa, A375, and PANC-1, and the non-malignant cell line MRC5. Cancer cells were incubated with increasing concentrations of CDs, and cellular viability was determined. Treating HeLa, A375, and PANC-1 cell lines with CDs showed significant suppression of the cancer cell line growth, which was dose-dependent (Fig. 8 a-d). Since we explained above that the black-carrot-derived CDs contain ACNs on the surface, these compounds are most likely responsible for the inhibitory effect on cancer cell growth. Moreover, literature data points out ACN's potential as an antitumor agent [29,80]. The latest research indicated that ACNs selectively inhibited the proliferation of cancer cells with little influence on healthy cells [81]. ACNs can induce terminal differentiation of tumor cells and block tumorigenesis [82]. Finally, these flavonoids promote the apoptosis and autophagy of cancer cells through an internal mitochondrial pathway and external death receptor pathway and can inhibit angiogenesis and metastatic migration [29].

Detailed analysis revealed that the ortho‐dihydroxyphenyl structure on the B‐ring in ACNs is the active site that inhibits tumor growth and metastasis [83,84]. The IC50 values that we found for HeLa and A375 were 0.0770 and 0.0530 mg/mL, respectively (Table 1 ).

On the other hand, the PANC-1 cell line showed a lower sensitivity to the CD treatment, which was reflected in a higher IC50 value. This result is unsurprising because pancreatic cancer is highly resistant to chemotherapeutic agents [85].

The IC50 was not reached for the PANC-1 cell line until the highest tested dose of NPs (0.211 mg/mL). The IC50 of PANC-1 cells was approximately four times higher than that of A375 and three times higher than that of HeLa cells. The selectivity index (SI) was calculated to predict the therapeutic potential of CDs. A favorable SI indicates a drug with efficacy against tumor cells that is more significant than the toxicity against non-malignant cells (MRC-5). The greater the SI value, the more selective the CDs are. SI values of more than two were considered a promising anti-cancer sample [86,87] and obtained only for A375 cells. CDs have also shown a significant inhibitory effect on HeLa cells, with a specific selectivity level, not reaching the criteria for promising anti-cancer agents. The results suggested that A375 cells are the most sensitive to CD treatment and show the highest selectivity.

Malignant melanoma, with a very high mortality rate, is difficult to treat because it is very aggressive, and patients often develop resistance to conventional drugs and show signs of tumor recurrence within 6–9 months of the treatment [88]. Moreover, the number of drugs to effectively treat melanoma is low. Therefore, developing new prospective drugs to inhibit melanoma cells' proliferation effectively and metastasis is urgent. Our results imply that CDs are a promising anti-melanoma agent.

Besides those mentioned above, metastatic dissemination is the main cause of melanoma cancer lethality and represents an enormous challenge in cancer treatment [88]. Therefore, approaches to monitor cell migration are particularly relevant when studying the effect of novel anti-cancer drugs on metastatic progression. Since CDs showed excellent anti-cancer properties on the A375 cell line, we tested the effect of CDs treatment on A375 cell migration using an in vitro wound healing assay. Representative images of cell migration at 24 h time-point are shown in Fig. 9 a. A wound area's relative closure was compared between the treated and untreated cells to analyze the invaded area after 24 h.

The results were expressed as a percentage of wound closure, considering the area measured at zero time as 0% (Fig. 9b). The significantly slower cell migration of the A375 cells treated with CDs compared to the untreated cells (Fig. 9a,b) was found, which suggests that CDs reduce the migration rate compared to the untreated cells. Since the migratory behavior reflects metastatic characteristics, it is evident that CDs have the potential to inhibit A375 cell dissemination and tumor spreading.

ce:section.ce:section-title: Black carrot-based CDs intracellular imaging by fluorescence microscopy
ce:section.ce:para: In the next set of experiments, we first collected fluorescent microscopy images of CDs dispersed on a coverslip to demonstrate the potential of biomass-derived CDs for fluorescent imaging of cells. The fluorescent image in Figure SI4 shows that CDs emit a strong green fluorescence. Then, we explored the applications of CDs in A375 fixed-cell imaging (Fig. 10 ). Cell nuclei in non-treated, control, and CDs-treated A375 cells were stained with DAPI for easier visualization. Photomicrographs of the DAPI-stained control cells are shown in Fig. 10a-d. In control cells, blue-stained cell nuclei were observed in the blue channel (Fig. 10a), while the fluorescence signal in the green channel was not visualized with a 20 × magnification (Fig. 10b). Only discrete autofluorescence in the cell nucleus was detected when a 100-times magnification was used in the green channel (Figure S5). Fig. 10 shows the A375 cells treated with CDs at 20-fold (Figure e-h) and 100-fold magnifications (Figure i-l). The images on the first panel show cell nuclei stained with DAPI in the blue channel, the pictures in the second panel show an intense fluorescence signal of CDs at the green channel, the third panel shows inverted DAPI staining and the images on fourth panel show the images merged.

Upon the cell treatment with CDs, a solid green fluorescence appeared in the cytoplasm and the nucleus, thus demonstrating their ability to cross both the plasma and nuclear membranes and stain the whole cell (Fig. 10f,h). Many parameters affect cellular uptake, such as size, shape, surface charge of nanoparticles, and cell type. Our CDs nanoparticles have a diameter of 30 nm, a spherical shape, and a positive surface charge. Therefore, it is necessary to consider the influence of all these parameters on cellular uptake. According to the literature, the majority of in vitro investigations indicate that the highest cellular uptake occurs within the range of 10–60 nm, irrespective of the core composition or surface charge of nanoparticles [89–92]. Nanoparticles with a diameter between 30 and 60 nm can bind to enough cellular receptors to drive the membrane-wrapping process without a receptor shortage affecting endocytosis [93]. Secondly, the transport of nanoparticles across the membrane is related to the interaction between the charged particles and the cell membrane. The cell membrane is negatively charged, so it is not surprising that it can attract positively charged CDs, which further accelerates membrane transport. Meanwhile, the shape of nanoparticles also greatly affects their cellular uptake. Notably, reports suggest that the cellular uptake quantity of spherical nanoparticles is higher than that of a rod-like nanoparticles through endocytosis [94]. This observation is attributed to the belief that it takes more time for cells to encapsulate rod-like nanoparticles. As shown in Fig. 3., CDs have a spherical shape, which also contributes to fast uptake. Havrdova and co-workers also showed that positively charged CDs could enter the cells efficiently and precisely, and stain the cell nuclei and sub-nuclear compartments such as nucleoli [12,13]. Our results also suggest that CDs produced from biomass have a solid affinity to negatively charged DNA and RNA molecules, nucleus main compartments. The nucleic acid affinity of CDs is associated with the flavylium cation, originating from the specific chemistry of the black carrot extract, present on the surface of CDs that bind to negatively charged nucleic acids through an electrostatic bonding, π–π stacking, and H-bonding, respectively. Moreover, according to the fluorescent images, it is evident that some cells had a higher fluorescence rate than others when treated with CDs (Fig. 10f), cells marked with white arrows). We analyzed the cells with higher fluorescence rates. We observed that those cells with condensed chromatin in the mitotic phase of the cell cycle, i.e., certain stages of mitosis, ranging from late prophase to telophase, with the highest fluorescence intensity in metaphase cells (Fig. 10i, k and l).

Several research reports have shown that cationic CDs could be used to label cell nuclei, chromatin, and nucleoli [15,95,96]. Mainly, those CDs were biocompatible and exhibited strong fluorescence in the nucleus and almost no background fluorescence in the cytoplasm.On the contrary, our CDs show cytotoxicity on the cancer cells, internalization, good decentralization into the cytoplasm and nucleus, and the ability to monitor the cells in mitosis and proliferation.

The fluorescence photostability of dye is crucial for applications in cell imaging. Therefore, we assess the stability of the CDs after an extended storage period and during continuous UV irradiation. Notably, stored samples of CDs dyed fixed cells for ten months exhibited nearly identical emission spectrum intensities compared to those recorded on the first day (Figure SI.6). This finding highlights the long-term stability of CDs, even after extended storage periods. Additionally, Fig. 11 . illustrates the maximum emission intensity change of CDs under different UV lamp irradiation exposure times. CDs' aqueous solutions in triplicates were excited with 365 nm for 90 min at 30-minute intervals. The change of fluorescent emission intensity maximums was not statistically significant through 60 min, signifying the photostability of CDs across 1 hour of continuous exposure. Photobleaching was observed after 90 min of continuous UV irradiation.

These results indicate that CDs addressed in this report have good photostability, which makes them well-suited as fluorescent probes for cell imaging, and offer potential applications in conventional fluorescence microscopy analysis.

The synthesis process using black carrot extracts as precursors resulted in spherical CDs with homogeneous size and red fluorescence emission at 612 nm without dependence on the excitation wavelength. These black carrot-based CDs demonstrated high potential for fluorescence imaging of melanoma cells and can be used for image-guided follow-ups. Without further modification of their surface for specificity, the CDs were internalized and retained in the cytoplasm and nucleus, thus staining the whole cell. The cells with the highest fluorescent intensity were in the process of cell division, indicating that black carrot-based CDs could be potentially valuable as a highly stable fluorescent dye for cell visualization uptake and, simultaneously, as a proliferation marker for melanoma cells. In addition, black carrots-derived CDs demonstrated a high anti-cancer anti-metastatic potential and a high selectivity index for melanoma cells.

Strong red fluorescence was assigned to the specific surface of CDs, which is rich in -C=O/-C-OH groups responsible for the red emission, which we have explained by quantum chemical calculations. This work describes the emission as the origin, the first plausible explanation of the CD's fluorescence (depicted in Scheme 3). The results obtained in this study are significant because these low-cost and eco-friendly CDs synthesized from the natural product, black carrot biomass, have the potential to step forward in melanoma therapy, as this cancer type still has limited therapeutic options.
